lesson_title: Bit flags and bit manipulation via std::bitset

cards.text: To declare an 8-bit bitset, write: {{c1::std::bitset<8> mybitset{};}}
cards.source: O.1 - Bit flags and bit manipulation via std::bitset

cards.front: Why might you use bit flags instead of separate bool variables?
cards.back: To pack 8 Boolean values into a single byte for storage efficiency. A bool uses 1 byte (8 bits) but only needs 1 bit, wasting 7 bits.
cards.source: O.1 - Bit flags and bit manipulation via std::bitset

cards.text: std::bitset provides these key methods: {{c1::test()}} to query a bit, {{c2::set()}} to turn a bit on, {{c3::reset()}} to turn a bit off, and {{c4::flip()}} to toggle a bit.
cards.source: O.1 - Bit flags and bit manipulation via std::bitset

cards.question: What is the final value of bits in binary?
cards.explanation:
Start: 0000 0101
After set(3): 0000 1101 (bit 3 turned on)
After flip(4): 0001 1101 (bit 4 toggled from 0 to 1)
cards.source: O.1 - Bit flags and bit manipulation via std::bitset

cards.front: In the bit sequence 0000 0101, which bits are set (have value 1)?
cards.back:
Bits at position 0 and position 2.
(Bits are numbered right to left starting from 0)
cards.source: O.1 - Bit flags and bit manipulation via std::bitset

cards.back: An individual bit in an object used as a Boolean value (true/false, on/off, set/not set)
cards.source: O.1 - Bit flags and bit manipulation via std::bitset

cards.text: To check if bit 3 is set in a bitset, use: {{c1::bits.test(3)}} which returns {{c2::true}} if the bit is 1, {{c2::false}} if 0.
cards.source: O.1 - Bit flags and bit manipulation via std::bitset

cards.front: What type of integers should you use for bit manipulation?
cards.back:
Unsigned integers or std::bitset.
Bit manipulation is one of the few cases where unsigned integers are unambiguously preferred.
cards.source: O.1 - Bit flags and bit manipulation via std::bitset

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: reset(3) turns bit 3 off (sets it to 0), so test(3) returns false/0.
cards.source: O.1 - Bit flags and bit manipulation via std::bitset

cards.concept: When is bit manipulation commonly used
cards.explanation:
Bit manipulation is frequently used in:
  • Graphics programming
  • Encryption
  • Compression
  • Performance optimization
  • Storage-intensive applications
It's less common in general programming.
examples: std::bitset<8> flags{};  // 8 boolean flags in 1 byte
if (flags.test(2)) { ... }  // Check if flag 2 is set
cards.source: O.1 - Bit flags and bit manipulation via std::bitset

cards.text: When a bit changes from 0 to 1 or from 1 to 0, we say it has been {{c1::flipped}} or {{c1::inverted}}.
cards.source: O.1 - Bit flags and bit manipulation via std::bitset

cards.question: What is the final binary value?
cards.source: O.1 - Bit flags and bit manipulation via std::bitset

cards.text: A bit with value {{c1::0}} is said to be {{c2::false}}, {{c2::off}}, or {{c2::not set}}. A bit with value {{c3::1}} is said to be {{c4::true}}, {{c4::on}}, or {{c4::set}}.
cards.source: O.1 - Bit flags and bit manipulation via std::bitset
